exercises.exerciseTypeName: A: Write one word in each gap
exercises.title: A: Write one word in each gap
exercises.instructions: Write one word in each gap.
exercises.studentInstruction: Write one word in each gap.
exercises.passage: Many students dream (1) ...... studying at a prestigious university abroad. It can be a life-changing experience for those who (2) ...... the determination and drive to settle (3) ...... in an unfamiliar country. The challenge of adapting to a new academic culture is the feeling you get when you move somewhere so different from where you (4) ...... up that you are not sure how to behave. You can often be (5) ...... aback by how different the teaching style is in another country. Social customs and unwritten rules can vary enormously, and that can sometimes make it difficult to (6) ...... on with other students and to (7) ...... genuine friendships. They might not approve (8) ...... your study habits or might object (9) ...... ideas you express in class. You might even be banned (10) ...... using certain websites in some countries where internet access is restricted. (11) ...... you were to study in a country such as China, for example, you might find that some platforms are completely blocked. There, students can be forced (12) ...... use a VPN just to read international news. In time, though, most people who study abroad (13) ...... in love with their adopted country and come to appreciate its unique qualities. It does (14) ...... real courage to make such a significant leap, but many graduates agree (15) ...... the experience transforms you forever.

exercises.exerciseTypeName: B: Complete the sentences by changing the form of the word in capitals
exercises.title: B: Complete the sentences by changing the form of the word in capitals
exercises.instructions: Complete the sentences by changing the form of the word in capitals when this is necessary.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the sentences by changing the form of the word in capitals when this is necessary.

items.sentence: My (FRIEND) ...... with Carlos goes back to primary school and is still as strong as ever.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is relationship noun from FRIEND, so the correct answer is "friendship".

wrongOptions.friend: The root FRIEND cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "friendship".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of FRIEND that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: FRIEND → friendship: Change the word class to a noun with the right suffix.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "friendship" is the word-formation answer from the root FRIEND in this context.

items.sentence: Many teachers complain about their students' (OBEY) ......, but teenagers have always tested boundaries.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is opposite of obey, so the correct answer is "disobedience".

wrongOptions.obey: The root OBEY cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "disobedience".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of OBEY that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: OBEY → disobedience: Apply the correct suffix or word form from the root.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "disobedience" is the word-formation answer from the root OBEY in this context.

items.sentence: Stop being so insecure about other people's success – you're just (JEALOUS) ...... of them.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is feeling envious, so the correct answer is "jealous".

wrongOptions.jealous: The root JEALOUS cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "jealous".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of JEALOUS that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: JEALOUS → jealous: Apply the correct suffix or word form from the root.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "jealous" is the word-formation answer from the root JEALOUS in this context.

items.sentence: The whole audience watched (NERVE) ...... as the young violinist stepped onto the stage alone.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is adverb from NERVE, so the correct answer is "nervously".

wrongOptions.nerve: The root NERVE cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "nervously".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of NERVE that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: NERVE → nervously: Use the -ing form for an activity or gerund.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "nervously" is the word-formation answer from the root NERVE in this context.

items.sentence: We need to ensure that (ABLE) ...... visitors can access every area of the new museum.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is visitors with disabilities, so the correct answer is "disabled".

wrongOptions.able: The root ABLE cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "disabled".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of ABLE that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: ABLE → disabled: Apply the correct suffix or word form from the root.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "disabled" is the word-formation answer from the root ABLE in this context.

items.sentence: My neighbour is incredibly (ARGUE) ...... and seems to enjoy picking fights with everyone on the street.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is likes arguing, so the correct answer is "argumentative".

wrongOptions.argue: The root ARGUE cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "argumentative".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of ARGUE that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: ARGUE → argumentative: Apply the correct suffix or word form from the root.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "argumentative" is the word-formation answer from the root ARGUE in this context.

items.sentence: Hasn't anyone ever told you that it's very (POLITE) ...... to use your phone during a meal?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is negative of POLITE, so the correct answer is "impolite".

wrongOptions.polite: The root POLITE cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "impolite".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of POLITE that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: POLITE → impolite: Apply the correct suffix or word form from the root.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "impolite" is the word-formation answer from the root POLITE in this context.

exercises.exerciseTypeName: C: Complete the second sentence using the word given
exercises.title: C: Complete the second sentence using the word given
exercises.instructions: Complete the second sentence using the word given, so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence. Write between two and five words in each gap.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the second sentence using the word given, so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence. Write between two and five words in each gap.

items.sentence:
I promised my mum that I would be home before midnight. **made**
I ...... that I would be home before midnight.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is promise collocation, so the correct answer is "made my mum a promise".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "made my mum a promise", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose the pronoun that matches the antecedent.

items.sentence:
My parents said I could go out despite the fact that it was a school night. **let**
My parents ...... despite the fact that it was a school night.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is allow someone to do something, so the correct answer is "let me go out".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "let me go out", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose the pronoun that matches the antecedent.

items.sentence:
Margaret and I have had a disagreement and we're not speaking to each other. **out**
Margaret and I ...... so we're not talking to each other.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is quarrelled, so the correct answer is "have fallen out".

wrongOptions.fallen out: This form misses the auxiliary — present perfect needs have/has + past participle.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "have fallen out", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "have fallen out" is a phrasal verb or fixed chunk that collocates with the rest of the sentence.

items.sentence:
The new law was rejected by everyone who heard about it. **approval**
The new law didn't ...... when it was introduced.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is no one approved, so the correct answer is "meet with anyone's approval".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple is wrong when the sentence links past to now (for, since, already, yet, ever).
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links a past action to the present or unfinished time.

items.sentence:
I was feeling tired, so I changed the subject to avoid any conflict. **mood**
I wasn't ...... an argument, so I changed the subject.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is not willing to argue, so the correct answer is "in the mood for".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "in the mood for", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence:
Tina felt sad because she found it impossible to connect with anyone at summer camp. **inability**
Tina was depressed because ...... friends at summer camp.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is couldn't connect, so the correct answer is "of her inability to make".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "of her inability to make", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
'Would you mind opening the door for me?' Simon said to me. **me**
Simon ...... the door for him.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is polite request reported, so the correct answer is "asked me to open".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "asked me to open", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Indirect speech keeps statement word order — do not invert subject and auxiliary.

items.sentence:
Don't forget to look after your little brother while I'm at the shops. **care**
Make sure you ...... your little brother while I'm out.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is look after, so the correct answer is "take care of".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "take care of", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentenceAfter: the competition, I'd donate half the prize money to a local charity.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is second conditional (unreal present), so the correct answer is "won".

wrongOptions.A: "win" does not fit the second conditional pattern (if + past, would + infinitive).
wrongOptions.win: "win" does not fit the second conditional pattern (if + past, would + infinitive).
wrongOptions.B: "have won" does not fit the second conditional pattern (if + past, would + infinitive).
wrongOptions.have won: "have won" does not fit the second conditional pattern (if + past, would + infinitive).
wrongOptions.C: "will win" does not fit the second conditional pattern (if + past, would + infinitive).
wrongOptions.will win: "will win" does not fit the second conditional pattern (if + past, would + infinitive).

explanationContent.usefulTip: Name the conditional type first — that tells you which verb forms belong in each clause.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Second conditional: if + past simple, would + infinitive — an unreal or unlikely present/future situation.

items.sentenceBefore: If you run into Jessica at the party,
items.sentenceAfter: her to give me a call when she's free.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The context points to "tell" — real future condition.

wrongOptions.A: "you will tell" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "tell" is correct.
wrongOptions.you will tell: "you will tell" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "tell" is correct.
wrongOptions.C: "you would have told" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "tell" is correct.
wrongOptions.you would have told: "you would have told" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "tell" is correct.
wrongOptions.D: "tell to" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "tell" is correct.
wrongOptions.tell to: "tell to" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "tell" is correct.

explanationContent.grammarFocus: Possessive forms and possessive determiners show who something belongs to.

wrongOptions.B: "would bake" does not fit the third conditional pattern (if + past perfect, would have + past participle).

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The context points to "in case" — precaution.

wrongOptions.B: "unless" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "in case" is correct.

wrongOptions.C: "provided that" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "in case" is correct.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is first conditional, so the correct answer is "see".
